title: Costs, revenue & profit
summary:
## The big idea Market structures — perfect competition, monopoly, oligopoly — are just different stages on which the same two actors perform: **cost curves** and **revenue curves**. Learn their shapes and the single golden rule (**profit is maximised where MC = MR**) here, and every diagram in the next chapter assembles itself. ## Short run vs long run The **short run** is the period in which at least one factor of production (usually capital — the factory) is **fixed**; only variable factors (labour, materials) can change. In the **long run, everything** can change, including scale. The two periods have *different* laws driving costs — the most common A2 confusion, so pin it now: **short run → diminishing returns; long run → economies of scale**. ## Costs in the short run - **Total fixed cost (TFC)** — unchanged whatever output is (rent, insurance). Its average, **AFC = TFC/Q**, falls continuously as output spreads the overhead ('spreading the fixed cost'). - **Total variable cost (TVC)** — rises with output (materials, power, labour). - **TC = TFC + TVC**; **AC = TC/Q**; **MC = ΔTC/ΔQ** — the cost of **one more unit**. **Why the U-shapes?** The **law of diminishing returns**: adding more of a variable factor (workers) to a fixed factor (one kitchen) raises output by less and less — the third cook helps, the eighth just queues for the stove. Falling marginal product means **rising marginal cost**, so MC eventually climbs; AVC and AC follow it up, giving the familiar U.
**What each part means:** the **yellow MC** curve falls briefly, then rises steeply as diminishing returns bite. The **orange AVC** and **red AC** curves are U-shaped; AC sits above AVC by the (ever-shrinking) AFC. The two dots mark the geometry examiners check: **MC cuts both AVC and AC exactly at their minimum points**. The logic: while the marginal unit costs less than the average, it drags the average down; once it costs more, it pulls the average up — so they cross at the bottom.
## Costs in the long run With all factors variable, the firm picks its **scale**. The **LRAC** curve — an 'envelope' skimming the bottoms of all possible short-run AC curves — shows what happens as scale grows: - **Economies of scale** — LRAC **falls**. The internal sources (learn the list): **technical** (bigger, better machines; containerisation), **purchasing** (bulk-buying discounts), **managerial** (specialist managers), **financial** (cheaper borrowing for big firms), **marketing** (one ad campaign spread over millions of units), **risk-bearing** (diversification). - **Diseconomies of scale** — beyond some size LRAC **rises**: communication chains lengthen, coordination fails, workers feel anonymous and motivation slips. - **Minimum efficient scale (MES)** — the *lowest* output where LRAC reaches its minimum. A huge MES relative to market demand is why some industries (aircraft, utilities) support only a few firms — or one (natural monopoly, Unit 8). - **External economies of scale** — the *industry's* growth cuts every firm's costs: a pooled skilled workforce, specialist suppliers, infrastructure (why tech firms cluster). ## Revenue - **TR = P × Q**; **AR = TR/Q = price**, so the **AR curve is the demand curve** — always. - **MR** — the extra revenue from one more unit. In **perfect competition** the firm is a **price taker**: sell as much as you like at the market price, so **AR = MR = P** (horizontal). In **imperfect competition** the firm must cut price to sell more, and the cut applies to *all* units — so **MR lies below AR** (for a straight-line AR, MR falls twice as steeply). ## Profit — and the golden rule Economists' 'cost' already includes **normal profit** — the minimum return keeping the entrepreneur in this industry (an opportunity cost). Anything above it is **supernormal (abnormal) profit**; anything below drives exit. > **Profit is maximised at the output where MC = MR** (with MC rising). Intuition: if MR > MC, one more unit adds more to revenue than cost — make it. If MR < MC, the last unit lost money — don't. Only at MC = MR is there nothing left to gain. This single rule locates equilibrium output in *every* market-structure diagram; the price is then read **off the AR curve above** that output. **Do firms really profit-maximise?** Alternatives: **revenue maximisation** (output where MR = 0), **sales/output maximisation** (biggest output consistent with normal profit, AC = AR), and **satisficing** — acceptable profit plus a quiet life. The root cause is the **principal–agent problem**: shareholders (principals) want profit, but salaried managers (agents) who run the firm may prefer size, salary and status. Rich evaluation material. ## Why it matters MC = MR, AR = demand, MR below AR, U-shaped AC, LRAC and MES — these are the working parts of every Paper 3 calculation and Paper 4 diagram on firms. Drill them until drawing feels automatic.
## ⚡ Quick revision - Short run: ≥1 fixed factor → **diminishing returns** → rising MC → U-shaped AVC/AC; **MC cuts AVC & AC at their minima**; AFC falls throughout. - Long run: all factors variable → **LRAC**: economies (technical, purchasing, managerial, financial, marketing, risk) → **MES** → diseconomies (communication, coordination, motivation). - **External economies**: whole-industry growth cuts costs (clusters). - **AR = price = demand curve**; perfect competition: AR = MR (horizontal); imperfect: **MR below AR**. - **Normal profit is a cost**; supernormal = anything above. - **Golden rule: MC = MR** → output; price from AR above it. - Alternatives: revenue max (MR = 0), sales max (AC = AR), satisficing — **principal–agent problem**.

keyTerms.definition: Fixed costs don't change with output; variable costs do.
The change in total cost from producing one more unit.
In the short run, adding a variable factor to a fixed one eventually lowers marginal product, raising MC.
Falling long-run average cost as the scale of production rises.
Rising long-run average cost as a firm grows too large.
The lowest output at which long-run average cost is minimised.
Total revenue per unit (TR/Q), equal to price and to the demand curve.
The change in total revenue from selling one more unit.
Normal profit is the minimum to stay in the industry; supernormal profit is any excess above it.
Producing where MC = MR (with MC rising).

diagrams: **Short-run cost curves:** U-shaped AC and AVC, falling AFC, and an MC curve cutting AVC and AC at their minimum points.
**LRAC curve:** falling (economies), reaching MES, then rising (diseconomies); show short-run AC curves nested within it.
**Revenue curves:** horizontal AR=MR (perfect competition) vs downward AR with MR below it (imperfect competition).
**Profit maximisation:** mark output where MC = MR and read price off the AR curve above it.
tips: Explain the **U-shaped short-run cost curves via diminishing returns**, and remember **MC cuts AC and AVC at their minimums**.
Separate the **short run (diminishing returns)** from the **long run (returns to scale/economies of scale)** — a classic confusion.
State that **AR = the demand curve**, and that **MR < AR** whenever the demand curve slopes down.
Apply the **MC = MR rule** for profit maximisation on every market-structure diagram.
Bring in **alternative objectives** and the **principal–agent problem** for evaluation of the profit-max assumption.

revisionNotes:
# Costs, revenue & profit — quick revision

**Short run (≥1 factor fixed) — diminishing returns**
- TFC + TVC = TC; AC = TC/Q; MC = ΔTC/ΔQ; AFC falls continuously.
- Diminishing marginal product → MC rises → AVC, AC U-shaped.
- **MC cuts AVC and AC at their minimum points.**

**Long run (all factors variable) — scale**
- **LRAC** envelope. Falling = **economies of scale**: technical, purchasing, managerial, financial, marketing, risk-bearing.
- **MES** = lowest output minimising LRAC (big MES → few firms).
- Rising = **diseconomies**: communication, coordination, motivation.
- **External economies**: industry growth (skill pools, suppliers, clusters).

**Revenue**
- AR = TR/Q = **price** = the demand curve.
- Perfect competition: price taker → **AR = MR**, horizontal.
- Imperfect competition: **MR < AR** (price cut applies to all units).

**Profit**
- **Normal profit counts as a cost** (opportunity cost of staying); supernormal = excess.
- **Maximise where MC = MR** (MC rising); read price off AR.
- Alternatives: revenue max (MR = 0), sales max (AC = AR), satisficing → **principal–agent problem** (ownership vs control).

**Exam habits** — separate diminishing returns (short run) from economies of scale (long run); apply MC = MR before reading anything else off a diagram.
questions:
# Practice questions — Costs, revenue & profit **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 3 style — multiple choice **1.** A firm's total cost rises from \$5,000 to \$5,060 when output rises from 100 to 101 units. Its marginal cost is: - A — \$60 - B — \$50 - C — \$5,060 - D — \$50.10
▸ Show answer

A. MC = ΔTC/ΔQ = 60/1 = \$60. (\$50.10 is the new average cost.)

**2.** In the short run, average cost curves are U-shaped because of: - A — economies and diseconomies of scale - B — the law of diminishing returns - C — the principal–agent problem - D — rising fixed costs
▸ Show answer

B. With a fixed factor, marginal product first rises then falls, so MC falls then rises, dragging the averages into a U. Scale economies (A) are a long-run phenomenon — the classic trap.

**3.** A large supermarket chain obtains discounts by ordering stock in bulk and borrows at lower interest rates than small rivals. These are examples of: - A — technical and managerial economies - B — purchasing and financial economies - C — external economies of scale - D — diseconomies of scale
▸ Show answer

B. Bulk-buying = purchasing economy; cheaper credit = financial economy. Both are internal (from the firm's own size), not external.

**4.** For a firm facing a downward-sloping demand curve, marginal revenue lies below average revenue because: - A — the firm is a price taker - B — selling an extra unit requires cutting the price on all units sold - C — average revenue includes fixed costs - D — marginal cost is rising
▸ Show answer

B. The extra unit brings in its own (lower) price minus the revenue lost by discounting every other unit — so MR < AR whenever demand slopes down.

## Paper 3 style — data response > A bakery has fixed costs of \$400 per week. Its total variable costs are: 100 loaves — \$150; 200 loaves — \$250; 300 loaves — \$390; 400 loaves — \$590; 500 loaves — \$850. Loaves sell in a highly competitive market at \$2 each. **(a)** Calculate the bakery's average cost at 300 loaves. **[2]** **(b)** Calculate marginal cost per loaf between 300 and 400 loaves, and between 400 and 500 loaves. **[4]** **(c)** Using the MC = MR rule, explain the profit-maximising weekly output and calculate the profit made there. **[6]** **(d)** The baker is considering doubling the size of the premises. Assess the factors that will determine whether this reduces long-run average cost. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) TC = 400 + 390 = \$790; AC = 790/300 ≈ \$2.63. [2]

(b) 300→400: ΔTC = 590−390 = 200 → MC = \$2.00/loaf. 400→500: ΔTC = 850−590 = 260 → MC = \$2.60/loaf. [4]

(c) Price taker → MR = \$2. Expand while MC ≤ MR: at 400 loaves MC = \$2.00 = MR; beyond, MC (\$2.60) > MR. Output = 400 loaves. Profit = TR − TC = 800 − (400+590) = −\$190 — a loss; note it still beats producing 500 (loss \$250) and may beat shutting down in the short run since revenue (800) covers variable cost (590) with \$210 toward fixed costs. [6]

(d) Economies available: technical (bigger ovens), purchasing (bulk flour); but a small bakery may hit its MES quickly; diseconomies (supervision) unlikely at this size; demand must absorb higher output at \$2; financing costs. Judgement: depends where current output sits relative to MES and on market demand. [8]

## Paper 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain why a firm's short-run average cost curve and its long-run average cost curve are both typically U-shaped, but for different reasons. **[8]** **(b)** Assess the view that large firms always produce at lower average cost than small firms. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Short run: diminishing returns to the variable factor → rising MC. Long run: economies then diseconomies of scale along the LRAC envelope. The distinction is the point.

(b) For: internal economies (all six types) and the MES logic. Against: diseconomies beyond MES; X-inefficiency in comfortable giants; small firms can exploit flexibility, niches and low overheads; industry MES varies enormously (aircraft vs hairdressing). Judgement: true up to MES, industry-specific after.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain the profit-maximising rule MC = MR, and why economists treat normal profit as a cost. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether firms in the real world actually aim to maximise profit. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Marginal reasoning both sides of MC = MR; normal profit as the entrepreneur's opportunity cost — the return needed to stay put — hence 'supernormal' for anything above.

(b) Against pure profit-max: principal–agent problem → managerial objectives (revenue, growth, perks), satisficing, sales max for market share, public/ethical objectives (ESG); information limits make MC = MR unknowable precisely. For: competition and shareholder pressure push toward profit over the long run; takeover threat disciplines managers. Judgement: long-run profit orientation with short-run deviations.
